name: WASP-18 b
why: A massive 'hot Jupiter' — its deep, obvious, evenly-spaced dips are the textbook look of a big planet crossing its star.

name: WASP-121 b
why: An ultra-hot Jupiter. Deep, clean, repeating transits — one of the easiest kinds of planet to confirm by eye.

name: LHS 3844 b
why: A rocky world with an 11-hour year around a small red star. The dip is shallow but repeats like clockwork.

name: GJ 357 b
why: A nearby transiting super-Earth. A modest, regular dip — real, but you have to look.

name: Pi Mensae c
why: TESS's very first new planet — a mini-Neptune. The dip is tiny (~300 parts per million); catching faint ones like this is exactly where humans beat the software.

name: AU Mic b
why: An infant planet around a 23-million-year-old star. The young star's flares and spots make the real transit hard to pick out — a genuine challenge.

name: Algol
why: Two stars eclipsing each other. Notice the V-shape and the shallower SECOND dip halfway between — planets don't do that. This is the most common false alarm.

name: RZ Cassiopeiae
why: An Algol-type eclipsing binary. The dip is deep and V-shaped — far too deep for a planet, which barely dims its star.

name: U Cephei
why: Two stars taking turns blocking each other. A deep primary eclipse plus a secondary dip — the fingerprint of a binary, not a planet.

name: delta Librae
why: A bright eclipsing binary. The eclipse is enormous compared to any planet transit — a clear impostor.

name: Vega
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: 61 Cygni A
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: Sigma Draconis
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: Delta Pavonis
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: 82 Eridani
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: Fomalhaut
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: Procyon
why: No repeating dip — just the star's natural flicker and measurement noise. Correctly saying 'no planet' matters as much as finding one.

name: Vega (with injected planet)
why: A faint transit we injected into this real star's real noise on purpose. Catching these is how we measure the crowd's sensitivity to small, Earth-like planets — an injection-recovery test.
source: Real TESS data · TESS Sector 74 + injected signal

name: 61 Cygni A (with injected planet)
why: A faint transit we injected into this real star's real noise on purpose. Catching these is how we measure the crowd's sensitivity to small, Earth-like planets — an injection-recovery test.
source: Real TESS data · TESS Sector 55 + injected signal

name: Sigma Draconis (with injected planet)
why: A faint transit we injected into this real star's real noise on purpose. Catching these is how we measure the crowd's sensitivity to small, Earth-like planets — an injection-recovery test.
source: Real TESS data · TESS Sector 40 + injected signal
